title: Computational Modeling of Antibody-Antigen Complexes: PLM-Based and MSA-Based Approaches
summary:
Antibodies play a central role in the immune response by specifically recognizing and neutralizing antigens, and therapeutic antibodies have become major drugs for cancer and autoimmune diseases. However, their discovery still relies on extensive in vitro screening, and accurate computational modeling of antibody structures and antibody-antigen interactions can prioritize candidates, reduce experimental burden, and accelerate rational design. Despite recent advances in high-accuracy protein and complex prediction, a persistent performance gap remains for antibody-related tasks compared with general protein-protein interactions, limiting downstream design.
  This thesis investigates why antibody-related tasks are harder and proposes improvements along two complementary directions. First, we investigate protein language model (PLM)-based methods for antibody and antibody-antigen structure prediction. Using embeddings from multiple PLMs, our approach achieves the best CDR-H3 accuracy among compared PLM-based methods on antibody monomer prediction. Extending it to complex prediction does not generalize: without co-evolutionary signals between antibody and antigen, single-sequence PLM representations do not reliably identify binding interfaces.
  Second, we develop two MSA-based interventions for antibody-antigen complex prediction: MSA refinement, which combines CDR-focused filtering with depth recovery from a larger sequence database, and convergence-aware recycling, which selects a stable intermediate recycle state for final diffusion sampling. Together, these interventions provide consistent gains over the AlphaFold3 baseline on a held-out antibody-antigen test set. Because the methods modify MSA construction and recycling behavior rather than model parameters, they apply without retraining or weight access.

AI.tldr: This thesis investigates why antibody structure and interaction prediction is harder than general protein prediction, finding that PLM-based methods work for antibody monomers but fail for complexes (lacking co-evolutionary signals), then develops two MSA-based interventions (refinement and convergence-aware recycling) that improve antibody-antigen complex prediction over AlphaFold3 without retraining.
AI.motivation: Antibody discovery relies on extensive in vitro screening, which is costly and time-consuming. Computational modeling could accelerate this process, but antibody-related prediction tasks consistently underperform compared to general protein-protein interaction prediction, limiting downstream design applications.
AI.method: Two complementary approaches: (1) PLM-based methods using embeddings from multiple protein language models for antibody structure and antibody-antigen complex prediction; (2) MSA-based interventions including CDR-focused filtering with depth recovery from larger databases (MSA refinement) and convergence-aware recycling that selects stable intermediate states for diffusion sampling.
AI.result: PLM-based approach achieves best CDR-H3 accuracy among compared PLM methods for antibody monomer prediction but fails to generalize to complexes due to missing co-evolutionary signals. The MSA-based interventions provide consistent improvements over AlphaFold3 on held-out antibody-antigen test sets.
AI.conclusion: Antibody-antigen complex prediction requires co-evolutionary information that single-sequence PLM embeddings lack. MSA refinement and convergence-aware recycling offer effective, retraining-free improvements to existing models like AlphaFold3.

title: On a phenotype-structured Shigesada--Kawasaki--Teramoto model: Turing instability and pattern selection under fast phenotype switching
summary: The Shigesada-Kawasaki-Teramoto (SKT) model has become a classical modelling framework for studying spatial segregation and cross-diffusion-driven pattern formation in competing populations. This model assumes phenotypic homogeneity, but phenotypic variability persists within any population and can strongly influence both ecological and evolutionary dynamics. In this paper, we present a generalised phenotype-structured formulation of the SKT model that accounts for phenotypic variability. In this formulation, the competing populations are continuously structured across some phenotype state spaces. Population members move and compete in phenotype-dependent ways, and can also switch between different phenotype states. First we show how a form of the classical SKT model, wherein parameters are written in terms of continuous weighted averages of the phenotype-dependent functions of the generalised structured model, with weights given by the phenotype distributions of the two populations, can be obtained in the quasi-invariant regime of fast phenotype switching. Then, still assuming fast phenotype switching and extending classical Turing-like linear and weakly nonlinear analyses, we explore the conditions for the emergence of spatial patterns, identify a Turing-type bifurcation threshold leading to pattern formation, and investigate the nature of such a bifurcation (super- or sub-critical) as well as the stability of the patterned state. The results obtained make it possible to draw connections between phenotype-dependent model functions and the emergence of population-scale aggregate spatial dynamics, showing in particular how phenotype distributions can act as effective control parameters for Turing instability and pattern selection. These findings are complemented by numerical simulations, which validate the formal asymptotics and confirm the predictions of the pattern formation analyses.

AI.tldr: The paper generalizes the classical SKT model to account for phenotypic variability in competing populations, showing how fast phenotype switching recovers the classical SKT model and how phenotype distributions influence Turing pattern formation.
AI.motivation: The classical SKT model assumes phenotypic homogeneity, but real populations exhibit phenotypic variability that strongly influences ecological and evolutionary dynamics. The authors aim to bridge this gap by developing a phenotype-structured formulation.
AI.method: The authors develop a generalized phenotype-structured SKT model with continuous phenotype spaces, analyze it in the quasi-invariant regime of fast phenotype switching, and extend linear and weakly nonlinear Turing analyses to study pattern formation.
AI.result: The classical SKT model emerges from the generalized framework under fast phenotype switching. Phenotype distributions act as effective control parameters for Turing instability and pattern selection. Numerical simulations validate the analytical predictions.
AI.conclusion: Phenotype variability can be systematically incorporated into cross-diffusion models, and phenotype distributions play a key role in determining spatial pattern formation in competing populations.

title: Mixing Vector Model for Copolymer Inference via Mixed Integer Linear Programming
summary: A novel two-phase molecule inference framework, mol-infer, has recently been developed to infer chemical graphs with prescribed abstract structures and desired property values through mixed integer linear programming (MILP) under the two-layered model, with guaranteed optimality and exactness relative to the given learned prediction function and structural constraints. In this study, we extend this framework to copolymers by introducing a simple feature representation, called the mixing vector (MV) model. In the proposed model, a copolymer feature vector is represented as a convex combination of MILP-tractable monomer descriptors weighted by the mixing ratio of the constituent monomers. This representation does not require explicit sequence-class information and is therefore naturally compatible with MILP-based inverse design. Under this model, we construct prediction functions for several copolymer property datasets using artificial neural networks, reduced quadratic multiple linear regression, and random forests. The proposed representation achieves practically useful predictive performance across multiple physicochemical property datasets; in particular, the best test R^2 score exceeds 0.7 for nine of the ten datasets and exceeds 0.9 for six datasets. We also formulate a multi-monomer inverse-design problem under the MV representation with a prescribed mixing ratio and show that the resulting MILP instances remain tractable, even for three-monomer settings. Finally, we perform an external consistency check by re-evaluating the inferred candidates and comparing the re-computed property values with those predicted by the learned model. Overall, the proposed framework gives a tractable first step toward model-level exact inverse design of copolymers under the two-layered model.

AI.tldr: This paper extends the mol-infer framework to copolymers using a mixing vector (MV) model, where copolymer properties are represented as convex combinations of monomer descriptors. It achieves high predictive performance (R² > 0.7 on 9/10 datasets) and formulates tractable MILP-based inverse design for copolymers, enabling exact inverse design under the two-layered model.
AI.motivation: To extend the existing mol-infer framework (designed for small molecules) to copolymers, which are more complex due to their polymeric nature and lack of explicit sequence-class information. The goal is to enable exact inverse design of copolymers with desired properties.
AI.method: The paper introduces a Mixing Vector (MV) model, where a copolymer's feature vector is represented as a convex combination of MILP-tractable monomer descriptors weighted by the mixing ratio. Prediction functions are built using ANN, reduced quadratic multiple linear regression, and random forests. The inverse design is then formulated as a MILP problem under the MV representation.
AI.result: The MV representation achieves practically useful predictive performance: best test R² > 0.7 for 9/10 datasets and > 0.9 for 6 datasets. The MILP-based inverse design remains tractable even for three-monomer settings, and external consistency checks confirm the reliability of the inferred candidates.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework provides a tractable first step toward model-level exact inverse design of copolymers under the two-layered model, offering guaranteed optimality and exactness relative to learned prediction functions and structural constraints.

title: FPLIER: Federated Pathway-Level Information Extractor
comment: Accepted for publication at the ACM BCB '26 conference
summary: In transcriptomics, gene-set-aware factorization methods such as the Pathway Level Information Extractor (PLIER) are most effective when trained on large, heterogeneous expression compendia. Yet, many clinically relevant cohorts cannot be pooled into a single dataset due to privacy and governance constraints. We present FPLIER, a federated extension of PLIER that enables distributed training across multiple data holders while incorporating publicly available datasets. Through secure aggregation, FPLIER produces training updates algebraically equivalent to those of a centralized pooled-data approach while keeping expression data local. We evaluate FPLIER across multiple scenarios in two simulated consortia (from the K-CLIER and MultiPLIER studies) and demonstrate stable convergence. We further conduct a systematic analysis of membership inference attacks targeting both intermediate training statistics and the released model. Our results show that privacy risk is governed by the rank of the training expression matrix. Incorporating public data or reducing data dimensionality increases this rank, moving the system toward a full-rank regime in which training and non-training samples become indistinguishable to the attacker, and membership-inference performance approaches random guessing.

AI.tldr: FPLIER is a federated extension of PLIER that enables privacy-preserving distributed training of gene-set-aware factorization models across multiple data holders, with mathematical equivalence to centralized training and demonstrated resilience to membership inference attacks when operating in a full-rank regime.
AI.motivation: Gene-set-aware factorization methods like PLIER are most effective when trained on large, heterogeneous datasets, but many clinically relevant cohorts cannot be pooled due to privacy and governance constraints. The authors aim to enable distributed training across multiple data holders without sharing raw expression data.
AI.method: FPLIER extends PLIER to a federated setting using secure aggregation, producing training updates algebraically equivalent to centralized pooled-data approaches while keeping expression data local. The authors evaluate across simulated consortia and systematically analyze membership inference attacks.
AI.result: FPLIER demonstrates stable convergence across multiple scenarios. Privacy risk is governed by the rank of the training expression matrix; incorporating public data or reducing dimensionality increases rank, moving toward a full-rank regime where membership inference attacks perform near random guessing.
AI.conclusion: Federated learning for gene-set-aware factorization is feasible with strong privacy guarantees, and the rank of the training expression matrix is a key determinant of privacy risk, with full-rank regimes offering strong protection against membership inference.

title: Subcortical Shape Variations and Their Associations with Cognition Across the 8th Decade of Life. A Study in the Lothian Birth Cohort 1936
summary: The study of brain morphology changes in normal individuals may capture aspects of functionally-relevant brain aging not fully indicated by gross volumetry. Despite the important role of subcortical brain structures in cognition, the associations between their morphological trajectories and cognitive changes in aging have not been documented. We use neuroimaging, demographic, and cognitive data from a large longitudinal study of cognitive aging, the Lothian Birth Cohort 1936, to explore shape changes in subcortical brain structures of community-dwelling individuals across their 8th decade of life. We investigate the association of these changes with cognitive aging using ANCOVA and mixed linear model analyses. Subcortical shape changes were heterogeneous, with varied atrophy patterns across whole period. The hippocampus and the ventral DC experienced varied morphological deformations (from its baseline point) different in left and right hemispheres, while the thalami and globus pallidi shapes, for example, experienced a more uniform volume contraction, nearly symmetrical throughout different timelines. Changes in general cognition were mainly associated with inwards and outwards vertex displacements between the time-points.

AI.tldr: This longitudinal study of the Lothian Birth Cohort 1936 examined subcortical brain shape changes during the 8th decade of life and found heterogeneous atrophy patterns across structures (e.g., asymmetric hippocampal changes vs. symmetrical thalamic contraction), with general cognitive decline linked to both inward and outward vertex displacements.
AI.motivation: To explore how morphological trajectories of subcortical brain structures—beyond gross volumetry—are associated with cognitive changes in aging, an area not yet documented despite the known role of these structures in cognition.
AI.method: Used neuroimaging, demographic, and cognitive data from the Lothian Birth Cohort 1936; applied ANCOVA and mixed linear models to analyze subcortical shape changes and their associations with cognitive aging across the 8th decade of life.
AI.result: Subcortical shape changes were heterogeneous: hippocampus and ventral DC showed asymmetric deformations between hemispheres, while thalami and globus pallidi exhibited uniform, nearly symmetrical volume contraction. General cognitive changes were associated with both inward and outward vertex displacements over time.
AI.conclusion: Subcortical morphological aging is structure-specific and hemisphere-dependent, and shape changes—not just volume loss—are linked to cognitive decline in older adults.

title: Phylogenetic dynamics of MRCA ages and empirical moments of a Brownian trait
summary:
We study the temporal dynamics of the first two empirical moments of Brownian traits on phylogenetic trees. For a fixed tree, we characterize the distributions of their empirical mean and empirical variance across all lineages extant at any given time. In particular, we show that the variance of the empirical mean and the expected empirical variance are piecewise linear between diversification events.
  For lineage-homogeneous random trees, both the variance of the empirical mean and the expected empirical variance can be expressed in terms of the expected age of the most recent common ancestor (MRCA) of a uniformly sampled pair of extant lineages. In this representation, the expected MRCA age enters the two quantities with opposite signs, pointing to a structural opposition between the variance of the empirical mean and the expected empirical variance.
  For generalized birth-death processes with time-dependent speciation and extinction rates, we derive an explicit formula for the distribution of the MRCA age of a uniformly sampled pair of extant lineages. This yields integral expressions, at any time, for both the variance of the empirical mean and the expected empirical variance. In the constant-rate birth-death case, we further obtain closed-form expressions for the expected empirical variance and describe its asymptotic behavior in the supercritical, critical and subcritical regimes.

AI.tldr: This paper characterizes the temporal dynamics of empirical moments (mean and variance) of Brownian traits on phylogenetic trees. It shows these quantities are piecewise linear between diversification events, links them to expected MRCA age with opposing signs, and derives explicit formulas for birth-death processes.
AI.motivation: To understand how Brownian trait moments evolve over time on phylogenetic trees, particularly how empirical mean and variance behave across lineages as a function of tree structure and diversification dynamics.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis combining phylogenetic tree dynamics with Brownian motion trait evolution. Uses distributional characterization for fixed trees, expected MRCA age representation for random trees, and explicit formulas for generalized birth-death processes with time-dependent rates.
AI.result: (1) Variance of empirical mean and expected empirical variance are piecewise linear between diversification events. (2) Both quantities expressible via expected MRCA age with opposite signs. (3) Explicit formulas for MRCA age distribution under generalized birth-death processes. (4) Closed-form expressions for constant-rate birth-death case across supercritical, critical, and subcritical regimes.
AI.conclusion: The paper establishes a structural opposition between the variance of the empirical mean and the expected empirical variance, mediated by MRCA age, and provides tractable analytical results for both fixed and random trees under various diversification scenarios.

title: Stochastic network epidemic model and particle filter: General framework and application to influenza in Japan
summary: Parameter inference and state estimation in stochastic and partially observed biological systems remain major problems in mathematical biology. In this work, we introduce a two-dimensional lattice graph model for the spread of infectious diseases. Estimating states and parameters in graph-based stochastic epidemic systems is particularly challenging because of randomness and incomplete observations. To address these issues, we propose a particle filter based data assimilation framework for the sequential estimation of both model states and unknown parameters. Two methodologies are developed: one based on the number of infected agents and another based on partial spatial location's information of infected agents on a two-dimensional lattice. The performance of the two methods are firstly analyzed and validated using synthetic data, and the first method is then applied to influenza data collected from different prefectures in Japan between July 2024 and December 2025. One-week-ahead forecasting simulations are also performed using current weekly data. The findings highlight the effectiveness of the proposed PF framework for real-time epidemic monitoring, forecasting, and adaptive public health decision-making.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a particle filter framework for estimating states and parameters in stochastic epidemic spread models on 2D lattices, using either infection count data or partial spatial location data. Validated with synthetic data, the method is applied to influenza data from Japanese prefectures (July 2024–December 2025) and shows effectiveness for real-time epidemic forecasting.
AI.motivation: Parameter inference and state estimation in stochastic, partially observed biological systems (e.g., epidemic spread) remain challenging due to randomness and incomplete observations, motivating the need for robust sequential estimation methods.
AI.method: A particle filter (PF) based data assimilation framework is introduced for a two-dimensional lattice graph epidemic model. Two variants are developed: one using the number of infected agents, and another using partial spatial location information of infected agents on the lattice.
AI.result: Both methods are validated on synthetic data. The first method is further applied to real influenza data from Japanese prefectures (July 2024–December 2025), with one-week-ahead forecasting performed using weekly data.
AI.conclusion: The proposed PF framework is effective for real-time epidemic monitoring, forecasting, and adaptive public health decision-making.

title: Lattice Brownian bees with cooperative reproduction: steady states, collapse, and spreading
summary: We extend the ``Brownian bees'' model of Berestycki et al. (2021, 2022) to cooperative reproduction, $kA\to(k{+}1)A$, of a population of $N$ symmetric random walkers with removal, at each birth event, of the particle farthest from the origin. Working in the limit $N\to\infty$, we formulate a hydrodynamic free-boundary problem for this model. Using this formalism, we determine steady state population densities for all~$k$ and prove their linear stability for $k\le 2$ and instability for $k\ge 4$. In the marginal case $k=3$, there is a whole continuous family of steady states at a single, critical ratio of the reproduction and diffusion rates. Above criticality the population undergoes an asymptotically self-similar finite-time collapse to the origin. Below the criticality the population spreads diffusively, but the reproduction remains quantitatively relevant. For $k\ge 4$, the unstable steady state separates regimes of a finite-time collapse and a diffusive spreading. Here the collapse dynamics is asymptotically self-similar, and the population density exhibits a scale separation requiring a matched-asymptotic description. Our analytical predictions are confirmed by numerical solutions of the hydrodynamic free-boundary problem and by Monte Carlo simulations of the original microscopic model.

AI.tldr: This paper extends the Brownian bees model to cooperative reproduction (kA → (k+1)A) in a population of N symmetric random walkers, analyzing steady states, stability transitions, and collapse/spreading dynamics via hydrodynamic free-boundary problems, with key findings for different k values including a critical case at k=3 and instability for k≥4.
AI.motivation: To understand how cooperative reproduction (requiring multiple particles for a birth event) affects the spatial and temporal dynamics of a population under a removal-at-birth constraint, extending prior work on the Brownian bees model which used simple binary reproduction.
AI.method: The authors formulate a hydrodynamic free-boundary problem in the N→∞ limit, analyze steady-state population densities, perform linear stability analysis, and validate analytical results with numerical solutions and Monte Carlo simulations.
AI.result: Steady states exist for all k, with linear stability for k≤2, instability for k≥4, and a marginal continuous family of steady states at k=3 at a critical ratio. Above criticality: finite-time self-similar collapse; below: diffusive spreading. For k≥4, unstable steady states separate collapse and spreading regimes requiring matched-asymptotic description.
AI.conclusion: The cooperative reproduction parameter k fundamentally alters population dynamics, producing phase transitions between stable, unstable, and marginal regimes, with self-similar collapse or diffusive spreading depending on parameters.

title: Training Ecosystems: A Computational Approach to Uncovering Learning Behavior in Unconventional Contexts
summary: Recent progress in diverse intelligence has shown simple learning capacities below the organism level - single cells and even molecular networks. However, there are still many knowledge gaps around learning capacity above the organism level, and about memory implemented purely by dynamical interactions without explicit memory media. We demonstrate that minimal ecological dynamics (in silico) are sufficient for several kinds of learning, assayed as changes in both, magnitude of response, and of recovery time. Systematic exploration of over 220,000 parameter combinations in a simulated classic predator-prey model revealed that, when perturbed by stimuli, recovery time exhibits habituation, sensitization, and a form of discrete number learning in a scale-invariant manner. Robustness analysis revealed that habituation and sensitization persist under stochastic perturbations, while discrete number learning is disrupted even at low noise levels. Dimensionality reduction revealed that the incidence of learning capacity is primarily determined by ecological interaction strengths. Clear, unique clustering patterns in parameter space allow high prediction accuracy for novel parameter combinations that enable learning. Response magnitude revealed a striking asymmetry: 90.6% of parameter combinations exhibited recovery time sensitization paired with habituation of response magnitude, while the opposite pattern was extremely rare. These findings highlight a set of phenomena at the intersection of ecology, basal cognition, and mathematics with many implications for a wide range of systems describable by similar kinds of equations. These properties provide numerous efforts in biology and engineering with a substrate that has considerable, pre-patterned, propensity for learning, which ultimately arises from mathematics, not depending on the details of physics or biology.

AI.tldr: Minimal ecological dynamics in a predator-prey model demonstrate habituation, sensitization, and discrete number learning through recovery time changes, with learning capacity determined by interaction strengths and showing notable asymmetry between response magnitude and recovery time.
AI.motivation: There are knowledge gaps about learning capacity above the organism level (ecological scales) and about memory implemented purely by dynamical interactions without explicit memory media. This study aims to fill these gaps.
AI.method: Systematic exploration of over 220,000 parameter combinations in a simulated classic predator-prey model, perturbed by stimuli, measuring changes in response magnitude and recovery time. Robustness analysis under stochastic perturbations and dimensionality reduction were performed.
AI.result: Recovery time exhibits habituation, sensitization, and discrete number learning in a scale-invariant manner. Habituation and sensitization persist under noise, but discrete number learning is disrupted. Learning capacity is determined by ecological interaction strengths. 90.6% of parameter combinations showed recovery time sensitization paired with habituation of response magnitude.
AI.conclusion: Minimal ecological dynamics are sufficient for learning, with properties arising from mathematics rather than details of physics or biology, offering a substrate for learning in diverse systems describable by similar equations.

title: Large language models reorganize representational geometry during in-context learning
summary: Large language models (LLMs) exhibit remarkable flexibility: they can adapt to novel tasks from in-context examples without any parameter updates, a capability known as in-context learning (ICL). Prior work on synthetic tasks has shown that ICL can implement specific algorithms, demonstrating architectural competence, and mechanistic analyses have identified key circuits that support this behavior. However, because in-context computation -- regardless of its algorithmic form -- relies on transformations in high-dimensional representation space, it remains unclear how the geometry of that space shapes ICL effectiveness. Motivated by the neuroscience view of classification as the untangling of neural representations, we hypothesize that ICL depends on the successful online untangling of task-relevant representations. To test this idea, we study how LLMs classify in-context examples whose labels are defined by the model's own internal representations with known structure. We show that ICL performance correlates systematically with the representational structure of the underlying classification task and that successful ICL is accompanied by geometric reorganization that increases online separability. We further find that LLM behavior is well described by a prototype-like algorithm that integrates evidence while reshaping representations to support classification. These findings offer a geometric account of ICL in pretrained LLMs, establish representational geometry as a mechanistic constraint on ICL, and quantify the gap between what pretrained representations afford and what in-context learning can exploit.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates how the geometric structure of internal representations in LLMs shapes in-context learning effectiveness, finding that successful ICL depends on online untangling of task-relevant representations and correlates with systematic geometric reorganization, with behavior explained by a prototype-like algorithm.
AI.motivation: While prior work shows ICL can implement specific algorithms and mechanistic analyses have identified key circuits, it remains unclear how the geometry of high-dimensional representation space shapes ICL effectiveness.
AI.method: The authors study how LLMs classify in-context examples whose labels are defined by the model's own internal representations with known structure, analyzing representational geometry and its reorganization during ICL.
AI.result: ICL performance correlates systematically with representational structure; successful ICL is accompanied by geometric reorganization that increases online separability; LLM behavior is well described by a prototype-like algorithm that integrates evidence while reshaping representations.
AI.conclusion: The findings offer a geometric account of ICL in pretrained LLMs, establishing representational geometry as a mechanistic constraint on ICL, and quantifying the gap between what pretrained representations afford and what in-context learning can exploit.

title: Molecular Lead Optimization via Agentic Tool Planning
summary: Drug discovery is a lengthy and resource-intensive process composed of multiple stages. Among these stages, lead optimization plays a critical role in transforming early hit compounds into viable drug candidates. This stage requires improving ADMET-related properties through subtle structural refinement while preserving key molecular substructures responsible for binding affinity to disease targets. Recent advances in artificial intelligence have shown promise in accelerating various aspects of drug discovery; however, most existing approaches to lead optimization rely on one-step molecular optimization, which fail to account for the long-term consequences of sequential design decisions. To address this limitation, we propose TRACE, a trajectory-aware, LLM-reasoning agent for molecular lead optimization that formulates tool selection as a sequential decision-making problem over action trajectories. Given a lead molecule and an optimization objective, TRACE makes trajectory-aware decisions over molecular optimization tools, enabling forward-looking refinement under structural constraints. Experiments on multiple ADMET optimization tasks show that our agent achieves higher optimization success, larger property improvements, and higher validity, while preserving molecular similarity compared to baseline models.

AI.tldr: TRACE is a trajectory-aware LLM agent for molecular lead optimization that formulates tool selection as sequential decision-making, achieving better ADMET property improvements while preserving molecular similarity.
AI.motivation: Existing approaches to lead optimization rely on one-step molecular optimization, failing to account for long-term consequences of sequential design decisions.
AI.method: TRACE uses an LLM-reasoning agent that makes trajectory-aware decisions over molecular optimization tools, enabling forward-looking refinement under structural constraints.
AI.result: TRACE achieves higher optimization success, larger property improvements, higher validity, while preserving molecular similarity compared to baselines.
AI.conclusion: Trajectory-aware sequential decision-making with LLM reasoning is effective for molecular lead optimization in drug discovery.

title: PROTOCOL: Late Interaction Retrieval for Protein Homolog Search
summary: Protein homology search underlies function annotation, structure prediction, and evolutionary analysis, but remains challenging in the "twilight zone," where global sequence similarity is weak and classical alignment methods lose sensitivity. Protein language models provide context-aware representations that could improve alignment sensitivity in this regime. However, prior protein embedding-based retrieval pipelines often pool these representations into a single vector, potentially obscuring local motifs, domains, or conserved residues that reveal remote homology. We introduce ProtoCol, a model which represents proteins as sets of residue embeddings and uses ColBERT-style late interaction to test whether residue-level comparison improves homolog retrieval. ProtoCol encodes proteins independently, keeps candidate representations pre-computable, and scores candidates with MaxSim over residue embeddings. On SCOPe superfamily and Pfam clan benchmarks, ProtoCol outperforms sequence-composition, alignment-based, pooled PLM, and trained single-vector baselines, supporting late interaction as an effective retrieval layer for remote homology search.

AI.tldr: ProtoCol uses residue-level late interaction (ColBERT-style) on protein language model embeddings to improve remote homology search in the twilight zone, outperforming alignment-based and pooled embedding methods on SCOPe and Pfam benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Protein homology search in the "twilight zone" (weak global sequence similarity) remains challenging for classical alignment methods, and prior embedding-based approaches pool representations into single vectors, losing local motif/domain information critical for detecting remote homology.
AI.method: ProtoCol encodes proteins as sets of residue embeddings (independent encoding), keeps candidate representations pre-computable, and scores candidates using MaxSim (late interaction) over residue embeddings, following a ColBERT-style design.
AI.result: On SCOPe superfamily and Pfam clan benchmarks, ProtoCol outperforms sequence-composition, alignment-based, pooled PLM, and trained single-vector baselines.
AI.conclusion: Late interaction serves as an effective retrieval layer for remote homology search, preserving fine-grained residue-level information that pooled representations lose.

title: Traditional machine learning vs. deep learning from dynamic graph representations of proteins' 3D folds in the task of protein structure classification
comment: Main paper: 16 pages, 4 figures, and 1 table; Supplementary information: 13 pages, 9 figures
summary: Protein structure classification (PSC) uses supervised learning to predict a protein's CATH/SCOP(e) class from the protein's sequence or 3D structural feature(s). We already modeled 3D structures as (static) protein structure networks (PSNs), demonstrating the competitiveness of PSN-based features to sequence or direct (i.e. non-network) 3D structural features in the PSC task. More recently, we demonstrated the power of features extracted from dynamic PSNs over features extracted from static PSNs (and thus by transitivity over sequence and direct 3D structural features) in the same task. That dynamic PSN approach used traditional machine learning (ML), combining manual (pre-engineered) features with an off-the-shelf classifier. Here, we evaluate whether automatic deep learning (DL) from the dynamic PSNs yields improvements. Our evaluation on 72 datasets spanning ~44,000 CATH- or SCOPe-labeled dynamic PSNs reveals that in terms of PSC accuracy, traditional ML and DL are (close to) tied for a large majority of the datasets, while DL is on average 10+ times slower. We are the first to evaluate traditional ML vs. DL in the dynamic PSN-based PSC task.

AI.tldr: This paper compares traditional machine learning (ML) with deep learning (DL) for protein structure classification using dynamic protein structure networks (PSNs). Results show ML and DL achieve near-identical accuracy across 72 datasets (~44,000 proteins), but DL is 10+ times slower.
AI.motivation: To evaluate whether automatic deep learning from dynamic PSNs improves upon traditional machine learning (which uses manual features) for protein structure classification, as prior work showed dynamic PSN features outperform static PSN, sequence, and direct 3D structural features.
AI.method: The authors compared traditional ML (pre-engineered features + off-the-shelf classifier) vs. DL (automatic feature learning) on 72 datasets comprising ~44,000 CATH- or SCOPe-labeled dynamic PSNs for the PSC task.
AI.result: Traditional ML and DL achieve comparable accuracy on most datasets, with DL being on average 10+ times slower. No significant advantage of DL over ML was found for dynamic PSN-based PSC.
AI.conclusion: For dynamic PSN-based protein structure classification, traditional ML is preferable to DL due to comparable accuracy and much lower computational cost. This is the first study to compare ML vs. DL in this specific task.

title: Neural-Behavioral Representation of Natural Whole-body Movement in Monkeys
summary: Understanding how cortical activity represents natural whole-body behaviors in primates remains challenging. Limited by the diversity of movements and inaccessibility of large-scale neural representation of whole-body kinematics, previous motor decoding studies focused on constrained tasks and limited limb movements. Here, we present a neural-behavioral recording and modeling framework for freely moving monkeys, combining large-scale epidural cortical signals from distributed sensory- and motor-related areas with synchronized multi-view motion capture through a custom-made data collection platform. We reconstructed whole-body monkey kinematics and learned a compact behavior prior using an autoregressive encoder-decoder model. Conditioned on neural signals, the model decoded accurate and realistic whole-body movement without explicit physical constraints. Our results provide a novel proof-of-concept approach for decoding natural whole-body movements in primates using large-scale intracranial neural activity.

AI.tldr: Researchers developed a framework combining large-scale epidural cortical recordings with multi-view motion capture to decode natural whole-body movements in freely moving monkeys using an autoregressive encoder-decoder model, achieving accurate and realistic kinematics without explicit physical constraints.
AI.motivation: Previous motor decoding studies were limited to constrained tasks and limited limb movements due to the diversity of movements and inaccessibility of large-scale neural representation of whole-body kinematics. Understanding how cortical activity represents natural whole-body behaviors in primates remained challenging.
AI.method: A neural-behavioral recording and modeling framework for freely moving monkeys, combining large-scale epidural cortical signals from distributed sensory- and motor-related areas with synchronized multi-view motion capture through a custom-made data collection platform. An autoregressive encoder-decoder model learned a compact behavior prior and decoded whole-body kinematics conditioned on neural signals.
AI.result: The model decoded accurate and realistic whole-body movement without explicit physical constraints, providing a novel proof-of-concept approach for decoding natural whole-body movements in primates using large-scale intracranial neural activity.
AI.conclusion: This work demonstrates that large-scale intracranial neural signals from distributed cortical areas can successfully decode natural whole-body behaviors in freely moving primates, offering a new approach for studying neural representations of complex movements.

title: Common Noise-Induced Group-Level Synchronization Between Uncoupled Groups of Oscillators
summary: We investigate group-level synchronization between oscillator groups induced by common noise in the absence of inter-group coupling. Each group receives a common noise shared by all its oscillators and independent local noise inputs to individual oscillators. The same common noise is applied to all groups. The system is studied with both identical and nonidentical oscillators, and with and without intra-group coupling. In the nonidentical case, natural frequencies are drawn from the same distribution for both groups, making them statistically equivalent. Through numerical simulations of this system, we find that the degree of synchronization within each group, measured by the absolute value of a complex Kuramoto order parameter, typically shows significant temporal fluctuations. Importantly, the complex order parameters representing the collective oscillations of the groups synchronize when the groups are driven by the same common noise. By deriving a phase density evolution mapping, we analytically explain how this group-level synchronization is achieved in the absence of intra-group coupling.

AI.tldr: This paper studies how groups of oscillators synchronize at the group level when driven by shared common noise, even without direct coupling between groups, and provides analytical explanation through phase density evolution mapping.
AI.motivation: To understand whether and how groups of oscillators can synchronize collectively when only sharing the same noise input, without inter-group coupling.
AI.method: Numerical simulations of oscillator groups with common noise (shared within and across groups) and local noise, for identical/nonidentical oscillators with/without intra-group coupling; analytical derivation via phase density evolution mapping.
AI.result: Group-level complex order parameters synchronize under common noise despite no inter-group coupling; intra-group synchronization shows significant temporal fluctuations.
AI.conclusion: Common noise alone can induce group-level synchronization, analytically explained by phase density evolution mapping.

title: Brain-IT-VQA: From Brain Signals to Answers
summary: Decoding visual content from fMRI signals recorded while a person views images, and specifically answering questions about the seen images, is a long-standing challenge. While significant progress has been made in recent years in visual question answering (VQA) from fMRI, performance remains limited. Moreover, although recent models can make increasingly accurate predictions, they have rarely been used as tools for understanding the structure of visual representations in the brain. We present Brain-IT-VQA, a framework for visual question answering from fMRI. Building on the Brain Interaction Transformer (Brain-IT), our method decodes language tokens from brain activity and integrates them with a language model to answer visual questions. Our model substantially outperforms previous fMRI-based captioning and VQA approaches. We further introduce NSD-VQA, a new dataset and benchmark for visual question answering from fMRI. Unlike existing image-fMRI VQA datasets, which typically provide only a few broad and weakly controlled questions per image, NSD-VQA provides on average 20 question-answer pairs per image across 20 controlled question categories that disentangle multiple levels of visual understanding. This enables more reliable and interpretable evaluation despite limited fMRI test data. Together, Brain-IT-VQA and NSD-VQA provide both a strong predictive framework and a tool for studying brain representations. Using this benchmark, we quantify which forms of visual and semantic information can be reliably decoded from fMRI responses to natural images. We further analyze the contributions of different brain regions across question types.

AI.tldr: Brain-IT-VQA decodes visual question answering from fMRI signals, outperforming prior methods, and introduces NSD-VQA—a controlled benchmark with 20 question categories per image—to enable reliable evaluation and brain representation analysis.
AI.motivation: To improve visual question answering from fMRI and provide a tool for understanding brain's visual representations, as prior methods have limited performance and lack controlled evaluation benchmarks.
AI.method: Builds on Brain Interaction Transformer (Brain-IT) to decode language tokens from fMRI and integrates them with a language model for VQA. Also introduces NSD-VQA dataset with 20 controlled question categories per image for interpretable evaluation.
AI.result: Substantially outperforms previous fMRI-based captioning and VQA approaches; quantifies which visual/semantic information is decodable from fMRI and analyzes brain region contributions across question types.
AI.conclusion: Brain-IT-VQA and NSD-VQA together offer a strong predictive framework and a reliable tool for studying visual representations in the brain.

title: Embodied Virtual Reality Feedback Reshapes Neural Representations to Support Continuous Three-Dimensional Motor Imagery Decoding
comment: 28 pages, 7 figures, 3 tables. Submitted to Nature Biomedical Engineering. Data to be made available via Zenodo (DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.16047021)
summary: Continuous brain-computer interfaces (BCIs) that decode motion trajectories from imagined movement offer intuitive motor control, yet how feedback modality and longitudinal training shape neural representations and decoding performance remains poorly understood. We present the first systematic investigation of embodied virtual reality (VR) feedback during real-time 3D virtual limb control driven by motor imagery, across ten longitudinal sessions in ten participants. Performance was evaluated using three strategies: actual online performance (Fixed Decoder Generalisation, FDG), periodic retraining (Sequential Adaptive Training, SAT), and within-session upper-bound estimation (Within-Session Reconstruction, WSR). A CNN-LSTM decoder achieved within-session imagined movement correlations of r = 0.762 under VR and r = 0.672 under screen feedback. VR significantly outperformed screen feedback across all strategies and movement dimensions (improvements of 8.9-13.0%, all p <= 0.002, d = 1.42-2.05). This advantage persisted under fixed decoders without retraining, demonstrating that embodied VR feedback elicits inherently more decodable and generalisable neural representations. Linear mixed-effects modelling confirmed robust main effects of feedback modality and movement axis with no interaction. Neurophysiologically, VR produced stronger sensorimotor-parietal desynchronisation and enhanced motor-frontal functional connectivity, with pervasive anterior insula engagement across all frequency bands and increased superior parietal lobule coupling, paralleling patterns associated with real movement execution. These findings establish embodied spatial feedback as a key design principle for next-generation continuous BCIs targeting intuitive motor control and neurorehabilitation.

AI.tldr: Embodied VR feedback during motor imagery-based BCI decoding significantly outperforms screen feedback across all metrics (8.9-13.0% improvement), producing more decodable and generalisable neural representations with neurophysiological patterns resembling real movement execution.
AI.motivation: To investigate how feedback modality (embodied VR vs. screen) and longitudinal training shape neural representations and decoding performance in continuous BCIs for motor imagery, an area that remains poorly understood.
AI.method: Ten participants completed ten longitudinal sessions controlling a 3D virtual limb via motor imagery with either VR or screen feedback. A CNN-LSTM decoder was used, and performance evaluated via three strategies: Fixed Decoder Generalisation (FDG), Sequential Adaptive Training (SAT), and Within-Session Reconstruction (WSR).
AI.result: VR feedback achieved significantly higher decoding correlations (r=0.762 VR vs. r=0.672 screen), with 8.9-13.0% improvements across all strategies. VR elicited stronger sensorimotor-parietal desynchronisation, enhanced motor-frontal functional connectivity, and anterior insula engagement resembling real movement.
AI.conclusion: Embodied spatial feedback is a key design principle for next-generation continuous BCIs targeting intuitive motor control and neurorehabilitation.

title: Digitally enriching a screening population for pancreatic cancer using routine blood-based measures and clinical histories
summary: Earlier detection of pancreatic cancer is key to enabling wider access to curative treatment and reducing cancer deaths; however, screening is presently not viable. Latent indicators of pathology are evident in an individual's disease and blood test trajectories and may predict the development of pancreatic cancer. Longitudinal sequences of coded diagnoses and blood test values accrued by patients throughout their clinical interactions were used to train a custom Transformer-based neural network with a multi-head attention mechanism to predict risk of pancreatic cancer with a multi-year lead time and risk-stratify populations for targeted screening. The cohort comprised 6,017 adults with pancreatic cancer and 177,081 controls (overall median age 75, 45% female) with median 12 years (interquartile range 6.9-16.2) of medical history prior to pancreatic cancer diagnosis. External validation via leave-one-site-out, out-of-sample testing predicting pancreatic cancer 1-, 2-, and 3-years prior to diagnosis demonstrated mean area under the receiver operating characteristic of 0.837 (95% confidence interval 0.827-0.848), 0.797 (95% confidence interval 0.782-0.813), and 0.760 (95% confidence interval 0.745-0.776), respectively. Estimated pancreatic cancer risks were well-calibrated (calibration plot slope 1.08, intercept of -0.077; Brier score 0.025), and a Bayesian population pancreatic cancer prevalence update allows estimated cancer risk outputs to be transportable across settings. At testing, a screening threshold of >3.3% risk of pancreatic cancer in 1-year offered a diagnostic odds ratio of 18.2. Our work therefore lays the foundation for a first population-level digital enrichment tool to widen access to curative-intent management of pancreatic cancer.

AI.tldr: A Transformer-based neural network trained on longitudinal diagnoses and blood test data can predict pancreatic cancer risk 1-3 years prior to diagnosis with high accuracy (AUC 0.84 at 1-year), enabling potential population-level screening.
AI.motivation: Earlier detection of pancreatic cancer is critical for curative treatment access and reducing mortality, but current screening approaches are not viable for broad populations.
AI.method: A custom Transformer-based neural network with multi-head attention was trained on longitudinal sequences of coded diagnoses and blood test values from 6,017 pancreatic cancer patients and 177,081 controls (median 12 years of medical history). External validation used leave-one-site-out testing for 1-, 2-, and 3-year prediction horizons.
AI.result: Mean AUC of 0.837 (1-year), 0.797 (2-year), and 0.760 (3-year) prior to diagnosis; well-calibrated (slope 1.08, Brier 0.025); at >3.3% risk threshold, diagnostic odds ratio of 18.2.
AI.conclusion: This work lays the foundation for a first population-level digital enrichment tool to identify high-risk individuals for targeted pancreatic cancer screening, potentially widening access to curative-intent management.
